speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 29, 1995, and we are interviewing Adam Melcer, that's A-D-A-M, Melcer, M-E-L-C-E-R. My name is Nancy Boyman, N-A-N-C-Y, Boyman, B-O-Y-M-A-N. We're in Deerfield Beach, Florida. That's D-E-E-R-F-I-E-L-D, Beach in Florida. And this tape will be conducted in English. This is tape one. My name is Nancy Boyman, N-A-N-C-Y B-O-Y-M-A-N. Today is November 29, 1995, and we're conducting an interview with Adam Melcer, A-D-A-M, Melcer, M-E-L-C-E-R, in Deerfield Beach, Florida. That's D-E-E-R-F-I-E-L-D Beach, Florida, USA. Good afternoon. Would you tell us your name and spell it for us please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam Melcer, A-D-A-M M-E-L-C-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth? Was it the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same name. Except Adam, I had Abraham.

speaker: interviewer
text: For whom were you named.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was named after my mother's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is December 8, 1913.

speaker: interviewer
text: With whom do you share a birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I share a birthday with my wife and my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now, Adam?

speaker: interviewer
text: What city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father is Menachim, was a carpenter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately when he was born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in the Wlodawa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he live most of his life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he pass on?

speaker: interviewee
text: He passed on in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the cause of his death?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had heart failure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of person was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Average guy. He worked very hard. And that's all. I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther Faiga.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name if you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name, yes. It was Rotbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her-- about when was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the same, '85-- 1885.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she pass on?

speaker: interviewee
text: She passed away in 1942 in the Warsaw, when the Germans took her away in Warsaw. In 1942, it was a-- that time the Germans collected all the Jews in Warsaw for-- and they said they're going to camps. But as far as we know, she went to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a camp, and they killed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of person was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a very nice person. She had four children, and she was a-- a housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were-- You said there were four children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about each one of your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a older sister, Sarah, Polish name was [? Sala. ?] And she was all the time with us in Warsaw. And then they-- after the uprising, they took her also away. And she was in Majdanek, as far as I know. And she was killed in Majdanek--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in 19-- she was born in 1911.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and do you know when she died, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died in 1944. Maybe the end of '43. It was after the uprising in Warsaw. She was taken out from Warsaw and to Majdanek.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of person was your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a-- a nice working girl. And she was working when she go up in some stores, a saleslady in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she married?

speaker: interviewee
text: She got married in the Warsaw Ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall her marriage name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your other sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My other sister is-- was two years younger than I am, and her name was Genia. And she was taken away during the Yom Kippur action in Warsaw. And she probably went to Treblinka also. And then, never heard of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of person was she like?

speaker: interviewee
text: She also, she was a younger girl at that time. And she went to school, and at that time in Warsaw, she worked in a-- when the Germans came in, she worked in a shop near-- in Warsaw, in a German shop. But they took her away from the shop in Yom Kippur. Was a selection in Warsaw, and I never heard of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was a younger guy, Herschel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born two years younger than my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would be 1917.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1917, yeah. Was a very good looking boy, beautiful boy, tall for his age. And when we were in Warsaw in the ghetto, he was still in the ghetto with us. Later, when it was the-- the selection, and later the uprising, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had a letter that he is in Lvov, working as a mechanic in a German shop, with the cars. And he was all right, and later was no news, any news. It seems like when the war broke out with Russia, and the Germans start with the Russian war-- war, we didn't have any more news from him. He got killed in that time when the Germans and the Russians was fighting. He got someplace, he got lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about your wife, when you-- her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I met my wife when I was 17 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through a cousin of mine, which was killed later in the war. But she was a friend of my cousin, and I meet her this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your cousin tell you about your wife, this girl he was introducing you to?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went to-- to school together or something. I don't remember. All I know is we met. She was 15 years old, and I was 17. And we got married a couple years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Eula, ?] and in English you say Julia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wildman, Wildman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it please?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-I-L-D-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her birthday, December 8, 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Julia's occupation when you met her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I met her she was still in school. And she has no occupation really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you marry? What day?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your anniversary day?

speaker: interviewee
text: June 6-- July 6-- or June-- when was it? July 6, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your wedding like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a quiet wedding in the rabbi's offices. And no big deal.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you got married-- excuse me. Excuse me. After you got married, what did you then do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a printer. And we had a printing shop. I-- on [? Pawia ?] 31 in Warsaw. Had a couple people working for me. And this way, you make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any children before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- yes. My daughter was born in August 6, I think, or was it September? August or September. Wait a second. Let me think. October 6. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] October.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you present in Warsaw when she was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- when the war started, in September 1, they said on the radio that the Germans will kill all the Jews-- I mean, and then, on September 6, I left Warsaw with my brother-in-law. My brother-in-law-- her brother-in-law, my-- I know how you say it. And we left both for-- in direction of Russia. And we spent the 10 weeks with the Russians, on the Russian side of Poland, which Poland was annexed by-- the-- this part of Poland was annexed by the Russians. And we spent the 10 weeks. Then I heard that my wife give-- gave birth to the baby. And we both came back to Warsaw after 10 weeks being with the Russian side. With the Russian [INAUDIBLE]. And matter of fact, we lived in a hotel, which was owned by my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] family. And then, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we came back to Warsaw after 10 weeks. Of course, and this hotel was taken by the Russian general staff, military staff. And I had to make acquainted with a Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was not a general. It was a-- I don't know his rank, and he told me I shouldn't go to Warsaw because we are going to Warsaw, anyway, so you come with the army. But I didn't listen to him, and I went to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your family life before the-- before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a very nice family life. I was working and have a good business. And we were young people, and all we do is stay with the family. We live together with her parents in the same apartment that time. As a matter of fact, the printing was in the same place. As on the front it was a store, a front store on the street-- from the street. And in the back we had the big apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your pre-war address? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: My pre-war address? I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: The street address.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the printing?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [? Pawia 31. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. Rather traditional because my father and his brother had to work Saturdays. He worked in a big factory, which and mostly Polish people worked there. He was a carpenter, and has to work Saturdays there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Later on, he was on his own, and he built furniture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any particular hobbies or things that you did as a young man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was playing soccer, was my hobby. There is another thing. I was later on, when I was 13 years old, I finished school. And I went to work. And they work was I became an apprentice in the printing. And this where I learned my trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you join any community organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, I became a member of the union, printers union, which was dominated by the Jewish Bund, the Socialist Party, the Jewish Socialist Party. I became a member of the youth section, which was at Tuchow. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At the same time, I was a member in a Jewish choir in the big Warsaw synagogue, which I sang in this choir from 19-- from 13 years old until I was 17, as a soprano.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sing with anybody famous?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We sang with [INAUDIBLE] and-- was the other cantors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot the other names too. Koussevitzky, Moshe Koussevitzky. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you speak at home, in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We usually spoke my home Jewish and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And outside in the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: And outside in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever the need was for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience anti-Semitism in your school when you were a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I went to a Jewish school. So we have only Jewish children. And we-- the school was taught in Hebrew, mostly Hebrew. Translated Hebrew and Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about out on the street, walking on the street or going to parks? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we lived in the Jewish section, so I didn't have too much connection with the Polish population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your life as a schoolboy, what the day was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very simple. I had the school from morning to afternoon. And I have some homework, a very hard-working school. I mean, we-- we learned a lot. The school was from the Jewish community school. So only boys were there, not girls. It was more like a religious school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of kid were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was a good kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your goals as a young man, as a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I started, after 13 years old, after my bar mitzvah, as I said, I became a apprentice in a printing shop And I learned my trade there. I was a printer from that time on, always be a printer, always was a printer.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the Jewish community of the part of Warsaw you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was all different parties, different kinds of Jews. And it was very interesting, was a active Jewish community in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what ways were they active?

speaker: interviewee
text: Political and organizational activities.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the relationships between the Jews and the non-Jews where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, we didn't have too much contact with the non-Jews. But in-- was normal relationships.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Hitler's rise to power in 1933 affect your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not till the war. Of course, we heard about what's going on in Germany. That the Jews were expelled from Germany, Polish Jews were expelled from Germany. And we know that something was gonna happen at the end, but we wouldn't expect a war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you and your family begin to realize that this was a dangerous situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the last year before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family at any time, or you, make any-- any attempts to escape from the-- from Poland, to leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, as a matter of fact, a couple of friends of mine left for Israel at that time by illegal immigration. But having a printing shop on my own at that time, and everything was going right, we didn't-- we didn't plan to leave Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Warsaw Jewish community respond in any-- in any way, organized or not, to the news and events going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the news from the press, what was going on, but we couldn't do much to do anything about. Of course the Zionist organization say we should go, everybody, to Israel. But as you know, the borders are-- were closed to Israel. The British didn't let in no Jews to Israel. You had to smuggle-- smuggled to come in there. And as long as you have a good house, a good business, it's not easy to leave everything and family to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a member of any movement or organization at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, I was a member of the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], the Bund, which says it really didn't. Now, later I was working with [INAUDIBLE], the Zionist organization. And they-- they tried to send people to Israel. So I didn't move. I was still in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever a member of a military?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are any anti-Jewish actions or pogroms that took place in Warsaw before September 1, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No pogroms. Maybe some places in the, let's say-- in some places maybe there were some actions against the Jews, single Jews. But was not pogroms. The Jews wouldn't stand for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about September 1, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the beginning of the war. And then, we left Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what happened? How did it-- How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard airplanes flying. And just that time was the beginning of bombardment of Warsaw. But this was just the beginning. And then, we stayed there for a couple days. And we left Warsaw on the way to Russia. We were bombarded on the way, on the road. Some Russian airplanes came down, and a lot of people were on the road walking. But we came to-- to-- away from Warsaw. And that time was no Germans there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attempt to hide any of your belongings, or store up food, or make any provisions or hiding places?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no hiding, but we remember that-- I remember that in the beginning of the war, her father, my wife's father, was working with the-- with the rice import of China. And he-- he arranged that a big package of rice [? both-- ?] for both our house. Because we knew, in the beginning, maybe there no food to be get. So we had rice, and we had sugar in the house. So we had a little prepared for-- for the war time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any anti-Jewish actions that took place after, immediately after, September 1, 1939 within that period at the very beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In the very beginning, we have no anti-Jewish action. First of all, I was not in Warsaw for the first 10 weeks. But after I came back, we still have like separation between the Jews and the Polish population. Even before the ghetto was opened, a special ghetto for Jews, but it was no change to the Germans made a ghetto for the Jews. And the Jews who lived outside the Jewish borders have to move in-- into the ghetto. Then a little who troubled was the Polish people moved in in the Jewish places. And they were separate at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of the local non-Jews to the forming of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people were glad of it. It got rid of the Jewish and better buildings. Because they took over the buildings which the Jews owned outside the Jewish section of Warsaw.
